---
layout: post
title: "AI, Accountability and the Stuyvesant Spectator"
date: 2026-06-07
author: The SPR Editorial Board
image: /assets/images/articles/spectator.png
caption: Stuyvesant Spectator Logo | Used Under Fair Use Doctrine
categories: "local"
excerpt: "A friendly look at AI sourcing and journalistic policy through the Stuyvesant Spectator."
---

Artificial Intelligence or AI has been prominent in many of our everyday lives since [ChatGPT was made public in 2022](https://openai.com/index/chatgpt/) and companies began to integrate AI into various software systems. While there are potential benefits in the medical and technology fields, there are also many concerns regarding the accuracy and reliability of generative AI especially due to it being [prone to hallucinations](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/ai-hallucinations) and [mirroring human biases](https://learn.hms.harvard.edu/insights/all-insights/confronting-mirror-reflecting-our-biases-through-ai-health-care). This is why we believe it should not be used to blindly find and copy sources for research and journalistic purposes and is the focus of our investigation into the Stuyvesant Spectator.

Perhaps one of the best aspects of being a student at Stuy is our shared community. There is an [abundance of clubs](https://epsilon.stuysu.org/stuyactivities) and events for students to participate in and many opportunities that we are privileged to have. The Spectator, plays an important role in this sense, informing, entertaining and persuading students with a diverse set of staff writers and leadership. This is why it has earned the moniker of “The Pulse of The Student Body,” as it represents so many of the students at Stuyvesant with [clear editorial independence and strong writing](https://stuyspec.com/about/our-charter) across generations. The relationship between the student body and the Spectator, at least to us, is an important one.

This, like any relationship, requires trust. The Spectator has continually built this trust and [acknowledged where it can improve](https://stuyspec.com/article/response-to-the-letter-to-the-editor), and we hope that they will continue to do so, especially considering the importance of transparency in building trust. To uncover information regarding if and how student journalists at the Spectator use AI in their research we decided to use [metadata to uncover sourcing information](https://help.openai.com/en/articles/12627856-publishers-and-developers-faq). 

Our team wrote a python script that automatically checked articles dating back to the start of the last school year to see if any links in Spectator articles included [UTMs (Urchin Tracking Modules)](https://support.google.com/urchin/answer/28307?hl=en) that would indicate AI sourcing such as "utm\_source=chatgpt.com" or "utm\_source=gemini.google.com." These would appear at the end of urls that were linked in the article and overall we found 21 instances in the past two years, with none early last year, only ramping up recently, until we contacted the EiCs of the Spectator regarding the situation.

![][image1]

As shown in our graph, AI usage was sporadic but growing over time with notable rises in Volume 116, Issue 3 and Volume 116, Issue 8\. While there were multiple articles which only have one or two sources found by AI throughout the entire article, some have multiple in small sections, suggesting that authors used AI to find a cluster of sources to support a specific argument. After finding this information we sent two emails with the Spectator responding to the second one.

![][image2]

As we mentioned in the email, these links are not definitive proof that AI was used in the writing process or with ill intent. To us however, this is still concerning as AI should not be relied upon for sources and in some cases finding an angel to a story. AI systems are notoriously designed to please users and can, at times, reflect biases baked into their training data. The UTM data isn’t the problem but rather a symptom of a larger issue. While the sources largely check out, the habit of letting AI find data and sources, especially regarding decisive and current events, can gradually shape what angles you pursue and what evidence you notice.

In their correspondence with the SPR, the EICs noted that their web department immediately ran internal checks to locate and sanitize the flagged hyperlinks, confirming that they were existent. Crucially, they reaffirmed that The Spectator’s official writing guidelines strictly prohibit AI usage which we were happy to see. They have still not yet sanitized any links, but it is safe to assume that based on our current data, they either are doing so for current articles or have tightened down on AI usage in writing and sourcing. 

We thank the EiCs and writers of the Spectator for their honesty and compliance in our investigations and hope that this continues in the next school year. They have actively acknowledged the issue but there is still progress to be made at the Spectator and across other schools. As we expressed in our emails, we hope that clear and transparent policies will be made regarding how, when and if AI is ever used at student newspapers including the Spectator. While it would be impossible to check the search history and writing of every student journalist, tangible practices can be put in place today to prevent misuse in the future.

In the end, this isn’t a case of misconduct or scandal but a simple look into how modern technology is changing journalism and our lives as students and writers. The presence of AI-generated UTM markers doesn’t diminish the hard work of the Spectator staff, but shows how we must have our standards match the technology we choose to, or not to, use. 

Moving forward, the responsibility falls not just on the Spectator, but on all publications to establish rigorous, modern frameworks for technological accountability. For the Spectator to ensure that the "Pulse of the Student Body" remains a truthful and earned moniker, it has to grow and recognize where it draws the line on what is and isn’t acceptable regarding journalistic procedure. While we at the Stuyvesant Political Review review usually prohibit AI usage except in special cases, we understand that it is the Spectator’s decision on what they want to allow, but all that we ask is that they make it clear to us and the rest of the student body what exactly that is.
